title: Diffraction
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master diffraction for 9702 Physics. Understand how waves spread around obstacles, crucial for explaining phenomena like light through slits.
learningObjectives: Define and describe the phenomenon of diffraction, including its observable effects.
Identify and explain the essential conditions for significant diffraction to occur.
Describe and account for the diffraction pattern produced by plane waves passing through a single slit.

simpleExplanation.title: Diffraction in a Nutshell
simpleExplanation.summary: Diffraction is what happens when waves, like light or sound, bend or spread out as they pass around the edge of an obstacle or through an opening. It's a fundamental property of all waves, explaining why you can hear someone around a corner even if you can't see them. The amount of spreading depends on the wavelength of the wave and the size of the obstacle or opening.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine trying to squeeze a large, bouncy ball through a small doorway. It can't go straight through without changing its path. Similarly, waves bend and spread when they encounter an opening or obstacle comparable to their own 'size' (wavelength).
steps: Understand what diffraction is and its defining characteristics.
Learn the conditions for observable diffraction (wavelength vs. aperture size).
Familiarise yourself with single-slit diffraction patterns and how to sketch them.
Practise identifying diffraction in various contexts and solving related problems.

sections.content: Ever wondered why sound seems to bend around corners, or why streetlights appear to spread into hazy patterns at night? This isn't magic – it's **diffraction**, a fundamental property of all **waves**. In this lesson, we'll dive into how waves **bend** and **spread** as they encounter obstacles or apertures, uncovering the crucial conditions that make this phenomenon noticeable. Get ready to illuminate your understanding of wave behaviour!

At its core, **diffraction** is the phenomenon where waves spread out as they pass through an opening (an aperture) or around the edge of an obstacle. Instead of travelling purely in straight lines, the wave fronts appear to bend, allowing energy to propagate into regions that would otherwise be shadowed. This bending is a direct consequence of every point on a wavefront acting as a source of secondary spherical wavelets (as described by **Huygens' Principle**).

While all waves diffract, the effect is only **noticeable** or **significant** when a specific condition is met: the **wavelength (λ)** of the wave must be comparable to the **size of the aperture (a)** or the **obstacle (d)** it encounters.

*   **If λ ≈ a or λ ≈ d**: Significant diffraction occurs. For instance, sound waves (with wavelengths of metres) diffract easily around doorways, which are also metres in size. This is why you can hear someone speaking from around a corner.
*   **If λ << a or λ << d**: Diffraction is negligible. Light waves (with wavelengths of hundreds of nanometres) pass through large windows (metres in size) with barely any observable spreading, appearing to travel in straight lines. This is why you can't see around corners with light.

A classic example of diffraction in action is observed when monochromatic plane waves pass through a **single narrow slit**. Instead of a sharp, bright line on a screen, we see a distinctive diffraction pattern:

*   **Central Maximum**: A very bright, broad central band, significantly wider than the slit itself.
*   **Secondary Maxima**: Fainter, narrower bright bands located on either side of the central maximum, decreasing rapidly in intensity as you move further away.
*   **Minima**: Dark regions where destructive interference occurs, separating the maxima.

The **intensity** of the diffracted light is greatest at the centre of the central maximum and drops off sharply, oscillating through secondary maxima and minima. The width of this pattern is determined by the ratio of the wavelength to the slit width.

items: Diffraction is the bending or spreading of waves around obstacles or through apertures.
Significant diffraction occurs when the wavelength (λ) is comparable to the obstacle or aperture size (a or d).
For a single slit, the central maximum is the brightest and widest part of the diffraction pattern.
Secondary maxima are much less intense and narrower than the central maximum, rapidly decreasing in brightness.
The width of the central maximum is inversely proportional to the slit width: a narrower slit produces a wider central maximum.
The width of the central maximum is directly proportional to the wavelength: a longer wavelength produces a wider central maximum.

sections.question: Monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm passes through a single slit of width 0.2 mm. Describe the diffraction pattern observed on a screen placed far from the slit, and explain how the pattern would change if the slit width were reduced.
sections.solution:
1.  **Description of initial pattern**: A distinct single-slit diffraction pattern will be observed. This consists of a bright, wide central maximum, flanked by much fainter and narrower secondary maxima. Dark minima separate these bright bands. The intensity decreases rapidly for the secondary maxima further from the centre.
2.  **Change when slit width is reduced**: According to the conditions for diffraction, if the slit width ('a') is reduced while the wavelength ('λ') remains constant, the ratio λ/a increases. This leads to **more pronounced diffraction**. The central maximum would become significantly **wider** and the secondary maxima would also spread out further from the centre. The overall intensity of the pattern would decrease as less light passes through the narrower slit, but the *relative* intensity distribution would remain similar, just more spread out.

sections.content: A common mistake is confusing single-slit diffraction with double-slit interference. Remember, single-slit patterns have a broad, intense central maximum with rapidly decreasing secondary maxima, whereas double-slit patterns show equally spaced, relatively equally intense bright fringes (within an overall diffraction envelope).

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What are the key conditions for observable diffraction in 9702 Physics?
faq.a: For observable diffraction, the wavelength of the wave must be similar in magnitude to the size of the obstacle or aperture it encounters. Our premium free revision course and past papers provide detailed examples for 9702, helping you master this concept.

faq.q: How does a single-slit diffraction pattern differ from a double-slit interference pattern?
faq.a: A single-slit pattern features a very wide and bright central maximum, with significantly less intense secondary maxima that rapidly decrease in brightness. A double-slit pattern, however, produces fringes of roughly equal intensity (though this intensity is modulated by the single-slit diffraction envelope of each slit).

faq.q: Why is diffraction more noticeable with sound waves than with light waves in everyday situations?
faq.a: Sound waves have much longer wavelengths (typically metres) than light waves (hundreds of nanometres). Therefore, sound wavelengths are more often comparable to common apertures like doorways or corners, leading to easily observed diffraction, whereas light typically appears to travel in straight lines unless it passes through very small openings.
